subtopic_detail: Empiric antibiotic therapy for suspected infective endocarditis in an IVDU
stem: A 32-year-old man with active intravenous heroin use presents with 5 days of fevers, rigors and pleuritic chest pain. He is haemodynamically stable but unwell. Three sets of peripheral blood cultures have been drawn from separate sites. Bedside transthoracic echo shows a 9 mm mobile vegetation on the tricuspid valve with mild tricuspid regurgitation. Chest imaging shows multiple peripheral nodular opacities consistent with septic pulmonary emboli.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 112/68, HR 108, RR 22, SpO2 94% on room air, Temp 38.6 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: Multiple needle track marks both antecubital fossae; soft systolic murmur at lower left sternal edge increasing on inspiration; no peripheral stigmata of left-sided IE
data_table.Investigations: WCC 16.4 x10^9/L, CRP 184 mg/L, creatinine 88 micromol/L, lactate 1.6 mmol/L; troponin negative; ECG sinus tachycardia

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate initial antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Intravenous vancomycin with intravenous gentamicin empirically while cultures are pending, with daily levels and renal function checks
options.rationale: AU empiric therapy for suspected IE in an IVDU covers methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus with vancomycin and adds gentamicin for synergy until cultures and susceptibilities return. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: infective endocarditis)

options.text: Intravenous ceftriaxone 2 g daily as monotherapy, given its broad gram-negative cover and convenient once-daily administration in this presentation
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone monotherapy does not reliably cover MRSA, the dominant pathogen in IVDU-associated right-sided IE. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: infective endocarditis)

options.text: Defer all antibiotics for 48 hours to allow blood cultures to declare a pathogen before any empirical antibiotic exposure
options.rationale: Delaying antibiotics in a febrile patient with vegetations and septic emboli risks progression; empirical cover is started after cultures are drawn. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: infective endocarditis)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg twice daily with outpatient cardiology follow-up arranged for the following week
options.rationale: Oral therapy is inadequate for the induction phase of IE; intravenous therapy in hospital is required. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: infective endocarditis)

options.text: Intravenous benzylpenicillin 1.8 g six-hourly as a single agent based on streptococcal cover for community-acquired endocarditis
options.rationale: Benzylpenicillin alone misses MRSA and Staphylococcus aureus, the leading IVDU IE pathogens. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: infective endocarditis)

explanation.summary: Empirical therapy for suspected infective endocarditis in an IVDU covers Staphylococcus aureus including MRSA with vancomycin, with gentamicin added for synergy and gram-negative cover until cultures return. Three sets of cultures from separate venepunctures precede antibiotics where time allows. Transthoracic echo identifies right-sided vegetations; transoesophageal echo is indicated if TTE is non-diagnostic or left-sided IE is suspected. Surgical referral applies to large vegetations, heart failure or refractory sepsis.
key_points: IVDU-associated right-sided IE is most commonly Staphylococcus aureus including MRSA
Empiric IV vancomycin plus gentamicin covers MRSA and provides synergy until cultures return
Three sets of blood cultures from separate sites precede antibiotics where the clinical state allows
explanation.pearls: Septic pulmonary emboli with a tricuspid vegetation in an IVDU points to Staphylococcus aureus; start vancomycin with gentamicin once cultures are drawn.

subtopic_detail: Post-exposure prophylaxis after occupational HIV needlestick
stem: A 29-year-old surgical resident sustains a deep hollow-needle injury during arterial line placement in a known HIV-positive patient with an unsuppressed viral load on adherence-poor antiretrovirals. The injury was 40 minutes ago. The resident is healthy, on no medications and not pregnant. She has had a single hepatitis B vaccination course with documented anti-HBs above 100 mIU/mL six months ago. Her last hepatitis C and HIV screens at occupational health 3 months ago were negative.

data_table.Examination: Wound at left index finger pulp, bleeding milked and wound irrigated with saline
data_table.Investigations: Source patient HIV RNA above 100,000 copies/mL on last clinic visit; source HCV antibody negative, HBsAg negative

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Send source patient and worker baseline HIV serology and arrange to start any prophylaxis in the morning once the laboratory has reported results
options.rationale: PEP efficacy declines steeply beyond 24 hours; starting prophylaxis cannot wait for baseline serology when the source is high-risk. (Source: ASHM National PEP Guidelines)

options.text: Commence tenofovir disoproxil with emtricitabine and dolutegravir as a 28-day three-drug course now and arrange baseline and follow-up testing in parallel
options.rationale: High-risk occupational exposure to a high viral load source mandates three-drug PEP started as soon as possible, ideally within hours, for 28 days. (Source: ASHM National PEP Guidelines)

options.text: Give a single 50 mg dose of dolutegravir as a pragmatic monotherapy prophylaxis until source results return at the next clinic visit
options.rationale: Single-dose monotherapy is not endorsed PEP; three-drug regimens for 28 days are the standard. (Source: ASHM National PEP Guidelines)

options.text: Test only the exposed healthcare worker now and defer any prophylaxis until her own baseline serology is available the following day
options.rationale: Worker baseline serology is essential but does not delay PEP initiation in a high-risk exposure. (Source: ASHM National PEP Guidelines)

options.text: Reassure the worker that her hepatitis B vaccine response covers her, and arrange follow-up serology at 3 months without any antiretroviral therapy
options.rationale: Hepatitis B immunity does not protect against HIV; PEP is required for a needlestick from a viraemic HIV source. (Source: ASHM National PEP Guidelines)

explanation.summary: High-risk occupational HIV exposure (deep hollow-needle injury from a viraemic source) mandates three-drug PEP started as soon as possible, ideally within hours and within 72 hours, for 28 days. The Australian first-line regimen is tenofovir disoproxil plus emtricitabine with dolutegravir or raltegravir. Baseline HIV serology is taken in parallel and repeated at 6 weeks, 3 months and (if indicated) 6 months. Hepatitis B and C status of source and worker are also documented.
key_points: Three-drug PEP within 72 hours, ideally within hours, of high-risk HIV exposure
First-line AU regimen: tenofovir disoproxil with emtricitabine plus dolutegravir for 28 days
Follow-up HIV serology at 6 weeks and 3 months, with earlier symptomatic testing if seroconversion is suspected
explanation.pearls: PEP started within 4 hours and continued for 28 days reduces the risk of HIV seroconversion after needlestick by around 80 percent; tenofovir-emtricitabine plus dolutegravir is the AU preferred regimen.

sources.label: Australian Society for Infectious Diseases occupational exposure statement

subtopic_detail: Latent tuberculosis treatment in an asymptomatic new migrant
stem: A 28-year-old man newly arrived from a high TB prevalence country attends a refugee health clinic for screening. He has no respiratory or systemic symptoms, no history of treated TB and no known contact with active TB. He is HIV-negative, his BMI is 22 and he has no liver disease. Quantiferon-Gold Plus is positive. Chest radiograph and clinical examination are unremarkable. Sputum induction and AFB testing have not been performed.

data_table.Examination: No lymphadenopathy, chest clear to auscultation
data_table.Investigations: QFT-Plus positive; CXR no infiltrate, cavitation or hilar adenopathy; ALT 22 U/L, AST 18 U/L, bilirubin 8 micromol/L; HIV serology negative; HbA1c 5.2%

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Commence a 6-month rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide and ethambutol regimen empirically as treatment for presumed active pulmonary tuberculosis
options.rationale: RIPE is for active TB; there is no clinical, radiological or microbiological evidence of active disease here. (Source: NTAC Position Statement on the Treatment of Latent TB Infection)
source_refs: NTAC Position Statement on the Treatment of Latent TB Infection

options.text: Provide no treatment at this stage and arrange a repeat Quantiferon assay in 12 months as routine follow-up for an asymptomatic positive result
options.rationale: Untreated latent TB has a 5 to 10 percent lifetime reactivation risk; recent migrants from high-prevalence settings benefit from preventive therapy. (Source: NTAC Position Statement on the Treatment of Latent TB Infection)
source_refs: NTAC Position Statement on the Treatment of Latent TB Infection

options.text: Offer 9 months of daily isoniazid with pyridoxine, or alternatively 4 months of daily rifampicin, with monthly clinical and liver function review during treatment
options.rationale: Latent TB preventive therapy with isoniazid for 9 months or rifampicin for 4 months reduces reactivation risk by 60 to 90 percent; AU programs accept either, with LFT monitoring. (Source: NTAC Position Statement on the Treatment of Latent TB Infection)
source_refs: NTAC Position Statement on the Treatment of Latent TB Infection

options.text: Collect three early-morning sputum samples for AFB smear and culture before deciding on any preventive or active treatment regimen for this man
options.rationale: Sputum AFB is for suspected active TB; without symptoms or CXR changes there is no indication. (Source: NTAC Position Statement on the Treatment of Latent TB Infection)
source_refs: NTAC Position Statement on the Treatment of Latent TB Infection

options.text: Repeat the Quantiferon-Gold Plus assay in 4 weeks before any decision is made, on the basis that early conversion windows may produce uncertain results
options.rationale: QFT-Plus does not require routine repeat for confirmation in an asymptomatic positive result with consistent risk factors. (Source: NTAC Position Statement on the Treatment of Latent TB Infection)
source_refs: NTAC Position Statement on the Treatment of Latent TB Infection

explanation.summary: Asymptomatic QFT-positive new migrants without radiological or microbiological active disease are offered preventive treatment for latent TB infection. AU options are 9 months of isoniazid with pyridoxine or 4 months of rifampicin; the rifampicin regimen has comparable efficacy with fewer hepatotoxicity events. Monthly clinical and LFT review during treatment is standard. Active TB must be excluded before preventive monotherapy.
key_points: Latent TB preventive therapy is offered after excluding active disease with symptoms screen and CXR
9 months isoniazid with pyridoxine or 4 months rifampicin are both accepted AU regimens
Monthly LFTs and symptom review screen for hepatotoxicity during preventive therapy
explanation.pearls: 4 months of daily rifampicin gives equivalent protection to 9 months of isoniazid in latent TB with lower rates of hepatotoxicity and better completion.

sources.label: NTAC Position Statement on the Treatment of Latent TB Infection
Communicable Diseases Network Australia: tuberculosis guideline

subtopic_detail: Hour-1 sepsis bundle in suspected urosepsis
stem: A 68-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes is brought to ED with 24 hours of fevers, rigors and worsening confusion. She has been treated by her GP for a urinary tract infection in the past week. On arrival she is tachypnoeic and hypotensive. Bedside venous blood gas shows pH 7.30 with lactate 4.2 mmol/L. Urinalysis shows leucocyte esterase, nitrites and blood. She has no documented allergies.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 84/52, HR 124, RR 28, SpO2 95% on room air, Temp 38.9 degrees Celsius, GCS 14
data_table.Examination: Cool peripheries with prolonged capillary refill, mild left renal angle tenderness
data_table.Investigations: Lactate 4.2 mmol/L, WCC 18.2 x10^9/L, creatinine 162 micromol/L (baseline 78), glucose 14.4 mmol/L; cultures and lipase pending

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Restrict fluids to a single 250 mL crystalloid bolus to avoid pulmonary oedema, then reassess clinically in 60 minutes before any further therapy
options.rationale: 30 mL/kg crystalloid in the first hour is recommended for septic shock; a 250 mL ceiling under-resuscitates. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021; AU sepsis pathway)

options.text: Withhold all antibiotics until blood and urine cultures have been processed by the laboratory, to optimise the diagnostic yield from microbiology
options.rationale: Antibiotics are given within 1 hour for septic shock; cultures are obtained before antibiotics where possible without delay. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021; AU sepsis pathway)

options.text: Commence noradrenaline by peripheral line at 0.1 microgram/kg/min before any crystalloid resuscitation, prioritising mean arterial pressure restoration first
options.rationale: Vasopressor support is escalated alongside or after initial 30 mL/kg crystalloid, not before any fluid. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021; AU sepsis pathway)

options.text: Crystalloid 30 mL/kg over the first hour, intravenous gentamicin plus piperacillin-tazobactam after blood and urine cultures, serial lactates and source imaging within the hour
options.rationale: The AU sepsis pathway delivers a balanced crystalloid 30 mL/kg, empiric IV antibiotics within 1 hour after cultures, and lactate trending; gentamicin plus piperacillin-tazobactam covers the likely organism for urosepsis pending sensitivities. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021; AU sepsis pathway)

options.text: Administer intravenous hydrocortisone 200 mg as the immediate first measure for circulatory collapse, plus a single dose of intravenous sodium bicarbonate to correct acidosis
options.rationale: Hydrocortisone is reserved for vasopressor-refractory shock; bicarbonate is not routine in sepsis with pH above 7.15. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021; AU sepsis pathway)

explanation.summary: The AU sepsis pathway and Surviving Sepsis Campaign hour-1 bundle for septic shock include lactate measurement, blood cultures before antibiotics, empirical broad-spectrum IV antibiotics within 1 hour, balanced crystalloid 30 mL/kg, and vasopressor escalation to target a MAP of at least 65. Source imaging and source control follow. Hydrocortisone is reserved for vasopressor-refractory shock. Empiric agents for urosepsis include gentamicin plus piperacillin-tazobactam.
key_points: Sepsis hour-1 bundle: lactate, cultures, IV antibiotics, 30 mL/kg crystalloid, vasopressors to MAP 65
Empiric urosepsis: gentamicin plus piperacillin-tazobactam pending culture sensitivities
explanation.pearls: Lactate above 4 mmol/L with hypotension defines septic shock; 30 mL/kg crystalloid plus broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour reduces mortality.

subtopic_detail: Empiric therapy for community-acquired bacterial meningitis
stem: A 34-year-old man presents to ED with 12 hours of severe headache, photophobia, neck stiffness and a single rigor. He has no recent travel, no immunosuppression and is on no regular medications. He is alert with no focal neurology. There is no rash. CT brain is reported normal. Lumbar puncture is being prepared by the ED registrar.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/82, HR 108, RR 18, SpO2 99% on room air, Temp 39.0 degrees Celsius, GCS 15
data_table.Examination: Kernig and Brudzinski signs positive, no rash, no papilloedema
data_table.Investigations: WCC 17.4 x10^9/L, CRP 142 mg/L, lactate 1.9 mmol/L, platelets 198 x10^9/L, INR 1.0; CT brain unremarkable

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate step?

options.text: Proceed straight to lumbar puncture with full opening pressure and CSF cell count, then hold all empirical antibiotics pending the laboratory report
options.rationale: Antibiotics and dexamethasone are not delayed for LP results; doses are given as soon as cultures are drawn. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: meningitis)

options.text: Administer intravenous ceftriaxone 2 g alone without dexamethasone, on the basis that steroids increase the risk of iatrogenic immunosuppression in young adults
options.rationale: Dexamethasone 10 mg given immediately before or with the first dose reduces neurological sequelae in pneumococcal meningitis. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: meningitis)

options.text: Start empirical intravenous acyclovir 10 mg/kg alone for presumed herpes simplex encephalitis, with bacterial cover deferred pending CSF PCR results
options.rationale: Bacterial meningitis empirical therapy is given immediately for any suspicion; HSV cover is added when encephalitic features are present. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: meningitis)

options.text: Defer all therapy until a contrast-enhanced MRI brain has been obtained and reviewed by a neurology registrar to characterise any meningeal enhancement
options.rationale: Imaging beyond non-contrast CT is unnecessary before LP and antibiotics in alert patients with no focal neurology. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: meningitis)

options.text: Give intravenous dexamethasone 10 mg with intravenous ceftriaxone 2 g now after a single set of blood cultures, then proceed to lumbar puncture
options.rationale: Empirical dexamethasone 10 mg given before or with the first dose of ceftriaxone reduces neurological sequelae; LP follows immediately. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: meningitis)

explanation.summary: Suspected bacterial meningitis is treated empirically without delay. In adults under 50 with no immunocompromise the AU regimen is IV ceftriaxone 2 g with IV dexamethasone 10 mg given before or with the first dose. Add benzylpenicillin for Listeria cover when over 50, immunocompromised or pregnant. CT brain is reserved for focal neurology, seizures or impaired conscious level; otherwise LP follows immediately after the first antibiotic dose.
key_points: Empiric IV ceftriaxone 2 g plus IV dexamethasone 10 mg given immediately after blood cultures
Add benzylpenicillin for Listeria cover if over 50, immunocompromised or pregnant
Pre-LP CT is reserved for focal neurology, seizures or impaired conscious level
explanation.pearls: Dexamethasone 10 mg IV given with or immediately before the first dose of ceftriaxone reduces neurological sequelae in pneumococcal meningitis when started before antibiotics.

sources.label: Communicable Diseases Network Australia: meningococcal disease
